text.title: Jenkins Basics Failure Modes
text.summary: 4 antipatterns observed in real Jenkins deployments. Each pairs symptom + root cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.symptom: Team writes new pipelines in Scripted Groovy because "it's more powerful". Linter cannot validate; restart-from-stage does not work; junior engineers cannot debug runtime errors.
antipattern.root-cause: Author copied a Scripted skeleton from a 2017 blog post without checking that Declarative covers the same use case.
antipattern.fix: Default to Declarative. Use `script { }` blocks only when Declarative has no equivalent (rare: dynamic stage generation, complex Groovy logic).

antipattern.symptom: Controller OOM-kills mid-build; job queue stalls; users see "Slave went offline" on the controller itself. Credentials store sees stray reads from build steps.
antipattern.root-cause: Controller has > 0 executors; some pipelines use `agent { label 'master' }` or `agent any` and land on the controller.
antipattern.fix: Set controller executors to 0 in JCasC. Force `agent { kubernetes ... }` or `agent { label '!master' }`. Forbid `agent any` via pipeline-policy lint.

antipattern.symptom: DR drill takes 6 hours of manual UI clicking to rebuild a controller. Config drifts between staging and prod; nobody knows what is canonical.
antipattern.root-cause: Operators configure Jenkins via the UI; JCasC is either not installed or not enforced.
antipattern.fix: Install JCasC. Store `jenkins.yaml` in Git. Periodically diff the running controller's exported config against the committed YAML; fail CI on drift.

antipattern.symptom: Pipelines that passed yesterday fail today after a plugin auto-upgrade. Sometimes the controller fails to start because two plugins are incompatible.
antipattern.root-cause: No `plugins.txt` pin; plugins are upgraded via the UI without testing in staging.
antipattern.fix: Pin every plugin in `plugins.txt` with `jenkins-plugin-cli`. Bump on a defined schedule; run the upgrade in a staging controller first; never auto-upgrade in production.
